explanation: This property specifies that whenever the ventilator is either in Start-up or VentilationOff (Standby) mode, the in valve must be closed, and the out valve must be open. We express this as a global constraint: globally, if StartUpMode or StandbyMode is true, then inValveClose and outValveOpen must both hold. We combine these with an AND in the 'then' clause and with an OR in the condition (mode selection). The globally operator captures the requirement that this must always be true when in those states, matching the original specification.

The input natural language specifies a requirement for two modes: Start-up and VentilationOff (also called Standby). When the ventilator is in either of these modes, the in-valve should be closed and the out-valve should be open. This means that whenever either StartUpMode or StandbyMode is true, inValveClose and outValveOpen should both be true. This is naturally captured by an invariance (globally) property: always, if (StartUpMode or StandbyMode), then (inValveClose and outValveOpen). The corresponding template for this implication is template 10 (G (bool_exp2 -> bool_exp3)), where bool_exp2 is 'StartUpMode | StandbyMode', and bool_exp3 is 'inValveClose & outValveOpen'. N_DURATION does not apply to this template.

The property requires that, whenever the ventilator is in Start-up mode or the Standby (VentilationOff) mode, the in valve must be set to close AND the out valve must be open. This is formalized using the always (globally) operator, so at every step in time and for all future states, if either StartUpMode or StandbyMode holds, both inValveClose and outValveOpen must also hold. This ensures safe configuration of the valves during these modes, meeting the intent of the requirement.

The property states that whenever the ventilator is in Start-up mode or in Standby/VentilationOff mode, then it must be the case that the in (inspiratory) valve is set to close AND the out (expiratory) valve is open. This property is always required to hold (globally), matching the intent that valve positions must always be enforced in those modes. We use the provided atomic propositions 'StartUpMode', 'StandbyMode' (for VentilationOff), 'inValveClose', and 'outValveOpen' to construct the Boolean expression. The implication is constructed so that at all times, if either Start-up or VentilationOff is true, then both inValveClose and outValveOpen are true. This accurately reflects the natural language requirement.

The requirement specifies that when the ventilator is in Start-up mode (StartUpMode) or Ventilation Off mode (StandbyMode), the inspiratory valve shall be closed (inValveClose is true), and the expiratory (out) valve shall be open (outValveOpen is true). This is a state-dependent safety constraint, and the LTL property should state that globally (at all times), if either StartUpMode or StandbyMode is true, then both inValveClose and outValveOpen must be true. The appropriate template is the implication form for two alternative conditions (StartUpMode | StandbyMode) implying the conjunction of the two required actuator states (inValveClose & outValveOpen).

The specification requires that whenever the ventilator is operating in either Start-up or VentilationOff (Standby) mode, the in valve should be set to close (inValveClose=true) and the out valve should be open (outValveOpen=true). This corresponds to a condition that must always hold while in those modes. Using the atomic propositions StartUpMode for start-up, StandbyMode for ventilation-off, inValveClose for the inspiratory valve being closed, and outValveOpen for the out valve being open, the property can be formalized as: Globally, if in StartUpMode or StandbyMode, then inValveClose and outValveOpen must both be true. This is captured by the template 'G (bool_exp2 -> bool_exp3)' with bool_exp2 = (StartUpMode | StandbyMode) and bool_exp3 = (inValveClose & outValveOpen).

The input natural language states that when the ventilator is in Start-up mode or Standby (VentilationOff) mode, the inspiratory (in) valve should be closed and the expiratory (out) valve should be open. This is interpreted as: if StartUpMode or StandbyMode is true (the ventilator is in one of these modes), then inValveClose and outValveOpen should both be true. Thus, the expression '(StartUpMode | StandbyMode) -> (inValveClose & outValveOpen)' is used. The global ('G') operator is selected to indicate that this constraint must hold at all times. This matches template 10: 'G (bool_exp2 -> bool_exp3)', with bool_exp2 as the mode condition and bool_exp3 as the desired valve state configuration. Number N_DURATION is not used for this template.

The property specifies that whenever the ventilator is in Start-up mode or StandbyMode (which is the same as VentilationOff mode), the in valve must be closed and the out valve must be open. This interpretation uses the atomic propositions: StartUpMode (ventilator in start-up mode), StandbyMode (ventilator in ventilation off/standby mode), inValveClose (in valve is closed), and outValveOpen (out valve is open). The formula asserts that globally, whenever StartUpMode or StandbyMode hold, it must be that inValveClose and outValveOpen both hold. This exactly captures the requirement expressed in the natural language, always linking the required valve states to the ventilator's operating mode.

The natural language says that when the ventilator is in Start-up or VentilationOff (StandbyMode), it is required that the in valve be closed and the out valve be open. In the atomic propositions, 'StartUpMode' and 'StandbyMode' are separate booleans that indicate the Start-up and Standby (VentilationOff) modes, respectively. The in valve closed is represented by 'inValveClose' and out valve open by 'outValveOpen'. 

This property should hold at all times: whenever StartUpMode is true or StandbyMode is true, then inValveClose and outValveOpen must both hold. Thus, the appropriate LTL template is a global (G) implication. The antecedent is 'StartUpMode | StandbyMode', and the consequent is 'inValveClose & outValveOpen'.

Template 10 corresponds to: G (bool_exp2 -> bool_exp3), and here:
bool_exp2 = (StartUpMode | StandbyMode)
bool_exp3 = (inValveClose & outValveOpen)

This fully captures the described requirement.

The requirement states that when the ventilator is in Start-up Mode or VentilationOff (Standby) Mode, the system must set the in valve to close and the out valve to open. This can be captured by checking, at all times, if the state is either StartUpMode or StandbyMode, then inValveClose must be true and outValveOpen must also be true. Thus, the condition should always be maintained whenever in Start-up or StandbyMode. This maps precisely to template 40, which expresses that globally, whenever (StartUpMode or StandbyMode) holds, then inValveClose and outValveOpen must hold. Bool_exp1 is 'StartUpMode', bool_exp2 is 'StandbyMode', bool_exp3 is '(inValveClose & outValveOpen)'. Not using bool_exp4. N_DURATION is not required for this template.
